title: Chapter 25: The Goodbye
content: I don't remember choosing to shift.

That's the thing that unsettles me most — not the arrival, but the absence of decision. One moment I was nowhere, suspended in the static between timelines, and the next I was here, standing on the cobblestones of Clonakilty with the salt wind in my hair and the late-afternoon sun bleeding gold across the storefronts.

The café is exactly as it was. Blue door, uneven frame, the hand-painted sign that reads BLAKE'S in letters that lean slightly to the right like they've had a few. The cats are in the window — both of them — curled around each other in a patch of sunlight, suspended in that particular feline indifference that makes the whole world feel temporarily okay.
I push open the door. The bell chimes. And Julian C looks up from behind the counter, where he's already making a latte.
He didn't ask. He just started making it when he heard the bell.
My chest does something complicated. Something architectural, like a wall folding in on itself.

"Avery," he says, and his voice is the same — low, warm, threaded with that gentle Cork lilt — but his face is different. The warm brown eyes still hold their kindness, the same kindness that made me trust him when I was bleeding on his floor, but there's something else behind them now. Depth. Layers. Like looking into a forest that goes back farther than you can see, farther than light can reach.
He sets the latte on the counter as I approach. Steam curls up between us.
"You look like you've seen a ghost," he says.
I almost laugh. I've seen three.
"Just a long walk," I say, and wrap my hands around the cup. The ceramic is warm and real and I hold on to it like a railing.

Julian C leans against the counter, arms crossed, studying me with that quiet attention he's always had — the one that makes you feel like you're the only person in the room even when the room is full. There's no one else here now. The café is empty except for us and the cats, and the silence has that particular quality that small Irish towns do so well: not empty, but held. Like the air itself is keeping a secret.

I take a sip. The latte foam is perfect — dense and sweet, painted with the vague shape of a heart that's either intentional or the most optimistic accident in the history of milk. Salt on my lips from the wind outside, mixing with the sweetness, and it tastes like everything I can't keep.
"Eamon's documents," I say. "They worked."
It's not a question, but he nods anyway. Something shifts in his jaw — the muscle there tightening and then releasing, like he's letting go of something he's been clenching for three years.

"Leaked to the press two days ago. All of it — the transactions, the shell companies, the payments from Osprey through the Dublin office." He says it quietly, almost under his breath, like speaking it too loud will summon the wrong attention. "Osprey's under formal investigation. Two of their directors have been called before the Oireachtas committee."
"And Harker?"

"Gone." A pause. "Pulled out. His employers got him out before the investigation could reach them — private flight from Cork Airport the night the story broke. Didn't even pack a bag, from what I hear." He shakes his head slowly. "Threw us to the wolves for three years and then vanishes like smoke the moment the fire reaches his door."

He says it without bitterness, which is somehow worse than if he were angry. Julian C swallowed his bitterness a long time ago. Dissolved it like a pill he had to take every morning just to keep breathing.
I set the cup down. "Julian."
He looks at me.

"For the first time in three years," he says, before I can say anything, "I'm potentially — and I need you to hear the *potentially* in that — not actively hunted. Not cleared. Not free. But—" He stops. His throat moves. "I might actually get my life back."
A beat.
"Or whatever's left of it."

The way he says it — soft, almost reverent, like he's handling something fragile — makes my eyes sting. Three years of running. Three years of looking over his shoulder. Three years of sleeping with one eye open in a tiny flat above a café in West Cork, and he's telling me this like he's afraid believing it will jinx it.

I want to tell him it won't. I want to tell him he's going to be fine. I want to tell him a lot of things, but the words keep running into the wall of what I actually know, which is that I don't know. I don't know if this timeline survives. I don't know if any of them do.
So I just nod. "Whatever's left of it is still pretty good."
He smiles — not the guarded one, not the one he wears like armor. The real one. The one that crinkles the corners of his eyes and makes him look his age instead of ten years older.

I move to the counter stools and sit, and within thirty seconds the cats have mobilized. Saoirse — a tabby with the exact coloration of an overcast sky and the dignity of a retired queen — lands in my lap with the particular heaviness of a cat who has decided you are furniture now. Gatsby, the orange monster, follows, draping himself across my thighs like a furry anchor.
"Traitors," Julian C says to them. "You don't even pay rent."
Saoirse's eyes close. Gatsby purrs loud enough to vibrate through my bones.

Julian C walks around the counter — the café is still technically closed, has been this whole time, I realize, but he never asked me to leave — and sits on the stool beside me. He reaches over to scratch Saoirse behind the ears, and his hand brushes my forearm.
The touch is small. Casual. The kind of thing that happens a hundred times a day between people who sit close to each other.
It's devastating.

My skin holds the warmth of it for a full three seconds after his hand moves back to the cat. I feel the ghost-print of his fingers like a brand, like a memory that hasn't happened yet, and I think: *I would choose this timeline.*
I thought it on the boat. I still mean it.
But now I know what choosing costs.

He's quiet for a while, both of us watching the light shift across the floor. Then he says, "Avery," and there's something careful in his voice, something approaching the edge of a confession. "I've been dreaming."
I go very still. "Dreaming?"

"Since the night you came here with the salt still on your coat and the stories about—whatever it was you said. The things in your life that don't add up." He's not looking at me. He's looking at the cats, at Saoirse's rhythmic breathing, at the dust motes turning slowly in the golden light. "I dreamed I was in a room with glass walls. And you were there, and you were crying. And a man in a suit was holding your face — not hurting you, but—containing you. Holding you like he had the right. And I—"
He stops. His jaw tightens.
"I wanted to kill him and thank him at the same time."
He laughs, but it's fragile. Thin ice over deep water. "I think I'm going a bit mad, Avery."
I take his hand. I don't think about it. My fingers find his like they've done it a thousand times in a thousand versions of this moment, and I hold on.
"You're not going mad," I say.
"Then what—"
"You're becoming."
I don't explain. I don't have the heart.

He looks at our hands — mine over his, his fingers curving up to meet mine, the fit of it natural and effortless and ruinous — and I watch him decide not to ask. He just holds on, and we sit there in the golden light of his café with the cats in my lap and our hands tangled together like rope, and for one perfect, impossible moment, I believe I could stay.
That's when I know I have to say goodbye.

Not with words. I'd never be that cruel to him — to tell him I'm leaving when I might not be. To make him grieve something that isn't gone yet. So instead I do the only thing I can think of that will mean goodbye without saying it.
I kiss him.

Not the way I kissed Julian B — not slow and deliberate, not careful and intentional. This kiss is urgent and tender and clumsy. I lean in and my hand finds the back of his neck and my mouth finds his and I kiss him like I'm trying to press everything I can't say directly into his skin. It tastes like salt and latte foam and the faint sweetness of the steamed milk on his lips, and under that, something clean and warm and specific to him — to this version of him — to the Julian who made me a latte without asking and let me sit in his closed café and never once demanded an explanation I couldn't give.
He makes a sound against my mouth. Quiet. Almost involuntary. Like something's been unlocked.
When I pull back, his eyes are wide. Not startled — *seen*. Like I've shown him a room in himself he didn't know existed.
"What was that for?" he whispers.
"For staying," I say. "For stitching yourself up on my table. For wanting to do the right thing even when it costs everything."

He looks at me. And his face — God, his face. He looks at me like I've given him something he didn't know he needed. Like I've answered a question he's been carrying for so long he forgot he was asking it.

The silence after the kiss is the loudest thing I've ever heard. The café holds it, the way it holds everything — gently, without judgment. The espresso machine hums. The cats purr. The light turns the color of honey left too long in the jar.
I stand. Gatsby protests with a chirping meow. Saoirse opens one eye, judges me, and closes it again.
"I should go," I say.
Julian C watches me from the stool. "You just got here."
"I know."
He doesn't argue. He never argues. He just accepts the things I can't explain, and that — that right there — is the most painful part.
I walk to the door. The blue paint is chipped near the handle. I've never noticed that before. I notice everything now, because noticing is all I have left.
I turn back.

The café is golden. The cats are sleeping, tangled together on the stool I just vacated. Julian C is behind the counter again, one hand resting on the espresso machine, the other raised in a half-wave — fingers spread, palm out, the most understated gesture of farewell I've ever seen. His face is lit with something I don't have a word for. Joy and confusion and tenderness and the beginning of something that looks, from here, an awful lot like hope.
I will carry that face forever. Whatever timeline I end up in. Whatever version of this story survives.
I open the door. The bell chimes.

The Irish air hits me like baptism. Cold and clean and smelling of rain coming in from the Atlantic, and the sea, and wet stone, and something underneath all of it — something that smells like home. Even though I'm not from here. Even though I wasn't born anywhere near this coast. It smells like home because he makes it home, and that's the whole problem, isn't it? The thing that makes a place worth choosing is the thing you have to walk away from.
I stand on the cobblestones. The sky is the color of pewter, heavy with the rain that's coming. The street is quiet. Late afternoon in a small town — everyone already where they need to be.
I open the app.
**INTEGRITY: 5%**
The number is smaller than I expected. Or maybe exactly as small as I feared.
Three paths pulse on the screen, glowing faintly like heartbeats:

**Wall Street.**
**Paris.**
**Clonakilty.**

I touch Clonakilty. My finger presses the name, and the screen brightens — the path illuminates, warm and golden, the same gold as the café light behind me. I hold it. I hold it for a long moment, feeling the warmth of the screen against my fingertip, feeling the word in my chest like a second heartbeat.
*Clonakilty.*
I let go.
The screen dims. The path goes back to pulsing, waiting, patient.
I haven't chosen. But I've said goodbye to one of them.
And the app, for the first time, doesn't give me a warning. No red text. No flashing urgency. No CAUTION or UNSTABLE or TEMPORAL DRIFT DETECTED.
It gives me something worse.
**CHOOSE.**
**THE CLOCKS ARE SYNCHRONIZING.**
**WHEN THEY ALIGN, ALL UNCHOSEN PATHS DISSOLVE.**
I stare at the screen until the words stop pulsing. Until they just sit there, stark and white, as unyielding as stone.

I kissed Julian C goodbye without telling him why. I held Clonakilty in my hand and felt its weight and set it down. The clocks are synchronizing — somewhere, in some mechanism I can't see, three timelines are ticking toward the same moment. And when they reach it, two of them will simply stop.
Not collapse. Not shatter. *Dissolve.* Like sugar in rain. Like a name you stop saying and eventually forget.
I have time. But not much.
I close the app and slide the phone into my pocket, and I stand on the street outside his café in the cold Irish afternoon, and I do the only thing left to do.
I breathe. And I start walking.
